title: Object lifetime and ownership

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Object lifetime and ownership; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An object's storage duration and lifetime determine when storage exists and values may be accessed; pointers do not extend the lifetime of the objects they designate.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A pointer to a block automatic array becomes dangling when execution leaves that block even if the address bits remain unchanged. Your goal is to identify owner, borrowers and release point for each resource in a routine. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic; Structures; Addresses and dereference; Scope, linkage and storage duration. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: An object's storage duration and lifetime determine when storage exists and values may be accessed; pointers do not extend the lifetime of the objects they designate. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Automatic, static, thread, and allocated storage have different start and end rules, and effective type or indeterminate values can invalidate access even while bytes exist. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Keeping a pointer variable alive keeps its pointed-to object alive as well.

includes: Relate automatic, static and allocated objects to valid reference lifetimes.
Identify owner, borrowers and release point for each resource in a routine.
excludes: Automatic, static, thread, and allocated storage have different start and end rules, and effective type or indeterminate values can invalidate access even while bytes exist.

outcomes: Identify owner, borrowers and release point for each resource in a routine.

examples.title: Object lifetime and ownership: defined case
examples.situation: A pointer to a block automatic array becomes dangling when execution leaves that block even if the address bits remain unchanged.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A pointer to a block automatic array becomes dangling when execution leaves that block even if the address bits remain unchanged.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Track object lifetime independently from pointer lifetime; a surviving pointer can be dangling after scope exit or free.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Track object lifetime independently from pointer lifetime; a surviving pointer can be dangling after scope exit or free.

examples.title: Object lifetime and ownership: boundary case
examples.situation: Allocated storage remains until free, but every pointer to it becomes invalid for access immediately after deallocation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Allocated storage remains until free, but every pointer to it becomes invalid for access immediately after deallocation.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Automatic, static, thread, and allocated storage have different start and end rules, and effective type or indeterminate values can invalidate access even while bytes exist.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Keeping a pointer variable alive keeps its pointed-to object alive as well.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Identify owner, borrowers and release point for each resource in a routine.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Keeping a pointer variable alive keeps its pointed-to object alive as well.
misconceptions.correction: Track object lifetime independently from pointer lifetime; a surviving pointer can be dangling after scope exit or free.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Keeping a pointer variable alive keeps its pointed-to object alive as well.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Object lifetime and ownership: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Automatic, static, thread, and allocated storage have different start and end rules, and effective type or indeterminate values can invalidate access even while bytes exist.
